lenses.guidance: 'Passover' translates Hebrew pesach (v 11) — the verb suggests both 'pass over' (LXX) and 'protect' (Akkadian cognate). The original audience hears protective presence, not mere bypass; God hovers, not just skips.
v 2's 'this month shall be the beginning of months' restarts Israel's calendar at deliverance. The narrative makes redemption not a date but the date — every month henceforth measured from this one.

The lamb 'without blemish' (v 5), kept four days and slaughtered at twilight (v 6), with blood applied to doorframes (v 7) — the pattern foreshadows Christ. Each instruction prefigures something specific in the gospel.

The substitution principle is established here: a lamb dies in place of the firstborn (vv 12-13). The redemptive arc that began with Genesis 22's ram now becomes liturgy — rescue at the heart of Israel's worship calendar.

v 46 prohibits breaking the lamb's bones — John 19:36 cites this exact regulation at the cross ('not one of his bones will be broken'). The Passover ritual's smallest detail predicts Christ's preserved body.

The Passover instituted here (v 14) is observed at Joshua's entry (Josh 5:10), Hezekiah's reform (2 Chr 30), Josiah's recovery (2 Kgs 23:21-23), and finally fulfilled at the Last Supper (Lk 22:7-20). A canonical thread.
